content:
# Luke Chapter 10 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 99-100 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_010_page_001.jpg, LUK_010_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And after these things the Lord appointed seventy-two others, and sent them two by two before his face into every city and place where he himself was about to come.

**2** And he was saying to them, "The harvest indeed is plentiful, but the laborers are few; therefore beseech the Lord of the harvest that he may send out laborers into his harvest.

**3** Go; behold, I send you out as lambs in the midst of wolves.

**4** Carry no purse, no bag, no sandals; and greet no one along the road.

**5** And into whatever house you enter, first say, 'Peace to this house.'

**6** And if a son of peace be there, your peace shall rest upon him; but if not, it shall return upon you.

**7** And in that same house remain, eating and drinking the things provided by them; for the laborer is worthy of his wages. Do not move from house to house.

**8** And into whatever city you enter and they receive you, eat the things set before you,

**9** and heal the sick in it, and say to them, 'The kingdom of God has drawn near to you.'

**10** But into whatever city you enter and they do not receive you, go out into its streets and say,

**11** 'Even the dust from your city that clings to our feet we wipe off against you; nevertheless know this, that the kingdom of God has drawn near.'

**12** I say to you that it shall be more tolerable for Sodom in that day than for that city.

**13** Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon which were done in you, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.

**14** But it shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon in the judgment than for you.

**15** And you, Capernaum, shall you be exalted to heaven? You shall be brought down to Hades.

**16** The one who hears you hears me, and the one who rejects you rejects me; and the one who rejects me rejects the one who sent me."

**17** And the seventy-two returned with joy, saying, "Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name."

**18** And he said to them, "I was beholding Satan having fallen like lightning from heaven.

**19** Behold, I have given you authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy; and nothing shall by any means harm you.

**20** Nevertheless, do not rejoice in this, that the spirits are subject to you; but rejoice that your names have been written in the heavens."

**21** In that same hour he rejoiced in the Holy Spirit and said, "I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding and have revealed them to babes. Yes, Father, for thus it was well-pleasing before you.

**22** All things have been delivered to me by my Father; and no one knows who the Son is except the Father, and who the Father is except the Son, and he to whom the Son wills to reveal him."

**23** And having turned to the disciples, he said privately, "Blessed are the eyes that see what you see.

**24** For I say to you that many prophets and kings desired to see what you see and did not see it, and to hear what you hear and did not hear it."

**25** And behold, a certain lawyer stood up, testing him, saying, "Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?"

**26** And he said to him, "What has been written in the law? How do you read it?"

**27** And he answered and said, "You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength and with all your mind, and your neighbor as yourself."

**28** And he said to him, "You have answered rightly; do this and you shall live."

**29** But he, wishing to justify himself, said to Jesus, "And who is my neighbor?"

**30** And Jesus, taking up the question, said, "A certain man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho, and he fell among robbers, who, having both stripped him and inflicted wounds, departed, leaving him half dead.

**31** And by coincidence a certain priest was going down on that road, and having seen him, he passed by on the other side.

**32** And likewise also a Levite, having come upon the place and having seen him, passed by on the other side.

**33** But a certain Samaritan, journeying, came upon him, and having seen him, was moved with compassion,

**34** and having come to him, he bound up his wounds, pouring on oil and wine; and having set him upon his own beast, he brought him to an inn and took care of him.

**35** And on the morrow, having taken out two denarii, he gave them to the innkeeper and said, 'Take care of him, and whatever more you spend, I, when I return, will repay you.'

**36** Which of these three do you think became a neighbor to the one who fell among the robbers?"

**37** And he said, "The one who showed mercy to him." And Jesus said to him, "Go, and you do likewise."

**38** And it came to pass, as they were journeying, that he entered into a certain village; and a certain woman named Martha received him into her house.

**39** And she had a sister called Mary, who also, having sat at the feet of the Lord, was listening to his word.

**40** But Martha was distracted with much serving, and having come to him, she said, "Lord, does it not concern you that my sister has left me to serve alone? Tell her therefore to help me."

**41** But the Lord answered and said to her, "Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things;

**42** but one thing is needful. And Mary has chosen the good portion, which shall not be taken away from her."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **10:1 — "seventy-two" (hebdomekonta duo):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΒΔΟΜΗΚΟΝΤΑ ΔΥΟ ("seventy-two") rather than the Byzantine ΕΒΔΟΜΗΚΟΝΤΑ ("seventy"). This is one of the most discussed textual variants in Luke. The Sinaiticus reading is followed as the earlier witness; the number likely alludes to the seventy-two nations of Genesis 10 (LXX) rather than the seventy elders of Numbers 11:16.

2. **10:1 — "appointed" (anedeixen):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΝΕΔΕΙΞΕΝ, meaning "appointed" or "publicly designated." This verb carries a more formal sense than a simple "chose," suggesting an official commissioning. The formal equivalent "appointed" is retained.

3. **10:11 — "that clings to our feet" (ton kollethenta... eis tous podas hemon):** Sinaiticus includes the fuller expression about dust clinging to the feet. The aorist passive participle ΚΟΛΛΗΘΕΝΤΑ ("having clung") is rendered with the present "that clings" for English readability while preserving the image.

4. **10:15 — "shall you be exalted?" (me heos ouranou hypsothese):** The Sinaiticus reading frames this as a rhetorical question with ME expecting a negative answer, followed by the declaration of descent to Hades. This interrogative form is preserved rather than the declarative found in some witnesses.

5. **10:17 — "seventy-two" (hebdomekonta duo):** Consistent with verse 1, Sinaiticus again reads ΕΒΔΟΜΗΚΟΝΤΑ ΔΥΟ at the return of the missionaries, confirming the manuscript's internal consistency on this reading.

6. **10:18 — "I was beholding" (etheoron):** The imperfect ΕΘΕΩΡΟΥΝ indicates continuous or visionary observation. Rendered "I was beholding" to preserve the imperfect aspect, suggesting an ongoing vision rather than a single momentary event. This theologically significant verb conveys Christ's divine perspective on Satan's fall.

7. **10:21 — "in the Holy Spirit" (en to pneumati to hagio):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΩ ΠΝΕΥΜΑΤΙ ΤΩ ΑΓΙΩ with the double article construction emphasizing the Holy Spirit's distinct personhood. This is a rare Lukan notation of Jesus rejoicing specifically in the Spirit, a significant Trinitarian moment.

8. **10:22 — "All things have been delivered to me":** This verse, sometimes called the "Johannine thunderbolt" for its resemblance to the Fourth Gospel's theology, is fully attested in Sinaiticus. The mutual knowledge between Father and Son (exclusive reciprocal knowledge) is rendered with formal precision.

9. **10:25 — "lawyer" (nomikos):** Sinaiticus reads ΝΟΜΙΚΟΣ, a term specifically denoting an expert in the Mosaic law. Rendered "lawyer" as the formal equivalent, distinguishing this figure from the ΓΡΑΜΜΑΤΕΥΣ ("scribe") used elsewhere.

10. **10:30 — "taking up the question" (hypolabon):** The participle ΥΠΟΛΑΒΩΝ, literally "having taken up" or "having received," indicates Jesus taking up the lawyer's question in reply. Rendered "taking up the question" to capture this nuanced verb of responsive engagement.

11. **10:31 — "by coincidence" (kata synkyrian):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΤΑ ΣΥΓΚΥΡΙΑΝ, a term appearing only here in the New Testament. Rendered "by coincidence" as the formal equivalent, though the narrative context invites the reader to question whether any coincidence truly operates under divine providence.

12. **10:33 — "was moved with compassion" (esplagchnisthe):** The verb ΕΣΠΛΑΓΧΝΙΣΘΗ derives from ΣΠΛΑΓΧΝΑ ("bowels, inward parts"), denoting a deep visceral compassion. This same verb is applied to Jesus elsewhere in the Gospels (7:13). Its application to the Samaritan draws a deliberate parallel with Christ's own compassion.

13. **10:35 — "two denarii" (duo denaria):** The ΔΥΟ ΔΗΝΑΡΙΑ represents approximately two days' wages for a common laborer, a substantial sum demonstrating the Samaritan's extraordinary generosity. The currency term is transliterated rather than converted.

14. **10:39 — "at the feet of the Lord" (pros tous podas tou Kyriou):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΥΡΙΟΥ ("Lord") rather than ΙΗΣΟΥ ("Jesus") found in some witnesses. The posture of sitting at the feet is the traditional posture of a disciple before a rabbi, indicating Mary's role as a learner of the word.

15. **10:41-42 — "Martha, Martha" and the "good portion":** Sinaiticus preserves the affectionate doubled vocative ΜΑΡΘΑ ΜΑΡΘΑ and reads ΤΗΝ ΑΓΑΘΗΝ ΜΕΡΙΔΑ ("the good portion"). The term ΜΕΡΙΣ ("portion") evokes the division of inheritance and the Levitical language of God as one's "portion" (Psalm 73:26; 119:57).

16. **10:42 — "one thing is needful" (henos de estin chreia):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝΟΣ ΔΕ ΕΣΤΙΝ ΧΡΕΙΑ, the shorter and likely more original reading. Some manuscripts expand to "few things are needful, or one" (oliga de estin chreia e henos). The Sinaiticus shorter reading is followed.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence maintained throughout:** Greek participial constructions are rendered as participles where English permits ("having come to him," "having taken out," "having sat at the feet"). The subordinate clause structures of Luke's Greek are preserved rather than being broken into simpler English sentences.

2. **Theological vocabulary preserved:** Key terms — kingdom of God (he basileia tou Theou), eternal life (zoe aionios), the Holy Spirit (to Pneuma to Hagion), Lord (Kyrios) — are rendered with established theological vocabulary. The title "Lord" (Kyrios) applied to Jesus at 10:1, 10:39, and 10:41 is consistently translated, reflecting Luke's high Christological usage.

3. **Sinaiticus priority:** Where Sinaiticus diverges from the Byzantine majority text, the Sinaiticus reading is followed. Most notably, "seventy-two" rather than "seventy" at 10:1 and 10:17, and the shorter reading at 10:42.

4. **Parabolic discourse register:** The Good Samaritan parable (10:30-37) is rendered in a narrative register appropriate to dominical teaching — vivid yet measured, with participial chains reflecting the Greek's progressive movement through the story. The three travelers (priest, Levite, Samaritan) are introduced with parallel grammatical structure to mirror the parabolic pattern.

5. **Imperfect tenses preserved:** Greek imperfect verbs indicating ongoing or repeated action are rendered with English continuous forms ("was saying," "was listening," "was beholding") to preserve the aspectual nuance of the source text, consistent with the approach in Luke 9.

6. **Discourse markers retained:** Luke's characteristic narrative connectors — "and it came to pass" (egeneto de), "and behold" (kai idou) — are preserved as formal equivalents, maintaining the Semitic-Greek literary quality of the Lukan narrative. The temporal marker "in that same hour" at 10:21 is rendered literally.

7. **Consistency with Luke 9:** The translation maintains continuity with the preceding chapter in vocabulary choices, narrative voice, and register. The commissioning of the seventy-two in chapter 10 echoes the sending of the twelve in 9:1-6, and parallel language is used to reflect this literary connection.
